question.text: An arbitrageur notices that gold futures are trading at Rs 53,000 per 10 grams while the spot price is Rs 50,000 and the 3-month cost-of-carry is Rs 1,500. What action should the arbitrageur take?

answer.text: Buy gold in the spot market and sell gold futures to earn Rs 1,500 riskless profit
Sell gold in the spot market and buy gold futures
Buy gold futures and wait for price convergence
No action — the pricing is fair

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Fair value = 50,000 + 1,500 = Rs 51,500. Futures at 53,000 > fair value. Arbitrage: buy spot at 50,000, sell futures at 53,000. On delivery, net gain = 53,000 - 50,000 - 1,500 = Rs 1,500 (riskless). **The Trap:** Selling spot/buying futures would work only if futures were underpriced. The pricing is NOT fair — futures are Rs 1,500 above fair value, creating an arbitrage opportunity. **Cross-Exam Context:** Cash-and-carry arbitrage is tested in Series VIII and I. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** In commodity markets, arbitrage also involves physical logistics — the arbitrageur must arrange warehousing and delivery, so actual profit may be slightly less than theoretical.
